{"content":"150: 	// WatchDog may be null if it's not needed/configured.
151: 	WatchDog *HealthzAdaptor
152: 
153: 	// ReleaseOnCancel should be set true if the lock should be released
154: 	// when the run context is cancelled. If you set this to true, you must
155: 	// ensure all code guarded by this lease has successfully completed
156: 	// prior to cancelling the context, or you may have two processes
157: 	// simultaneously acting on the critical path.
158: 	ReleaseOnCancel bool
159: 
160: 	// Name is the name of the resource lock for debugging
161: 	Name string
162: 
163: 	// Coordinated will use the Coordinated Leader Election feature
164: 	// WARNING: Coordinated leader election is ALPHA.
165: 	Coordinated bool
166: }
167: 
168: // LeaderCallbacks are callbacks that are triggered during certain
169: // lifecycle events of the LeaderElector. These are invoked asynchronously.
170: //
171: // possible future callbacks:
172: //   - OnChallenge()
173: type LeaderCallbacks struct {
174: 	// OnStartedLeading is called when a LeaderElector client starts leading
175: 	OnStartedLeading func(context.Context)
176: 	// OnStoppedLeading is called when a LeaderElector client stops leading.
177: 	// This callback is always called when the LeaderElector exits, even if it did not start leading.
178: 	// Users should not assume that OnStoppedLeading is only called after OnStartedLeading.
179: 	// see: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/127675#discussion_r1780059887
180: 	OnStoppedLeading func()
181: 	// OnNewLeader is called when the client observes a leader that is
182: 	// not the previously observed leader. This includes the first observed
183: 	// leader when the client starts.
184: 	OnNewLeader func(identity string)
185: }
186: 
187: // LeaderElector is a leader election client.
188: type LeaderElector struct {
189: 	config LeaderElectionConfig
190: 	// internal bookkeeping
191: 	observedRecord    rl.LeaderElectionRecord
192: 	observedRawRecord []byte
193: 	observedTime      time.Time
194: 	// used to implement OnNewLeader(), may lag slightly from the
195: 	// value observedRecord.HolderIdentity if the transition has
196: 	// not yet been reported.
197: 	reportedLeader string
198: 
199: 	// clock is wrapper around time to allow for less flaky testing
200: 	clock clock.Clock
201: 
202: 	// used to lock the observedRecord
203: 	observedRecordLock sync.Mutex
204: 
205: 	metrics leaderMetricsAdapter
206: }
207: 
208: // Run starts the leader election loop. Run will not return
209: // before leader election loop is stopped by ctx or it has
210: // stopped holding the leader lease
211: func (le *LeaderElector) Run(ctx context.Context) {
212: 	defer runtime.HandleCrashWithContext(ctx)
213: 	defer le.config.Callbacks.OnStoppedLeading()
214: 
215: 	if !le.acquire(ctx) {
216: 		return // ctx signalled done
217: 	}
218: 	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(ctx)
219: 	defer cancel()
220: 	go le.config.Callbacks.OnStartedLeading(ctx)
221: 	le.renew(ctx)
222: }
223: 
224: // RunOrDie starts a client with the provided config or panics if the config
225: // fails to validate. RunOrDie blocks until leader election loop is
226: // stopped by ctx or it has stopped holding the leader lease
227: func RunOrDie(ctx context.Context, lec LeaderElectionConfig) {
228: 	le, err := NewLeaderElector(lec)
229: 	if err != nil {
230: 		panic(err)
231: 	}
232: 	if lec.WatchDog != nil {
233: 		lec.WatchDog.SetLeaderElection(le)
234: 	}
235: 	le.Run(ctx)
236: }
237: 
238: // GetLeader returns the identity of the last observed leader or returns the empty string if
239: // no leader has yet been observed.
240: // This function is for informational purposes. (e.g. monitoring, logs, etc.)
241: func (le *LeaderElector) GetLeader() string {
242: 	return le.getObservedRecord().HolderIdentity
243: }
244: 
245: // IsLeader returns true if the last observed leader was this client else returns false.
246: func (le *LeaderElector) IsLeader() bool {
247: 	return le.getObservedRecord().HolderIdentity == le.config.Lock.Identity()
248: }
249: 
250: // acquire loops calling tryAcquireOrRenew and returns true immediately when tryAcquireOrRenew succeeds.
251: // Returns false if ctx signals done.
252: func (le *LeaderElector) acquire(ctx context.Context) bool {
253: 	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(ctx)
254: 	defer cancel()
255: 	succeeded := false
256: 	desc := le.config.Lock.Describe()
257: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
258: 	logger.Info("Attempting to acquire leader lease...", "lock", desc)
259: 	wait.JitterUntilWithContext(ctx, func(ctx context.Context) {
260: 		if !le.config.Coordinated {
261: 			succeeded = le.tryAcquireOrRenew(ctx)
262: 		} else {
263: 			succeeded = le.tryCoordinatedRenew(ctx)
264: 		}
265: 		le.maybeReportTransition()
266: 		if !succeeded {
267: 			logger.V(4).Info("Failed to acquire lease", "lock", desc)
268: 			return
269: 		}
270: 		le.config.Lock.RecordEvent("became leader")
271: 		le.metrics.leaderOn(le.config.Name)
272: 		logger.Info("Successfully acquired lease", "lock", desc)
273: 		cancel()
274: 	}, le.config.RetryPeriod, JitterFactor, true)
275: 	return succeeded
276: }
277: 
278: // renew loops calling tryAcquireOrRenew and returns immediately when tryAcquireOrRenew fails or ctx signals done.
279: func (le *LeaderElector) renew(ctx context.Context) {
280: 	defer le.config.Lock.RecordEvent("stopped leading")
281: 	ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(ctx)
282: 	defer cancel()
283: 	logger := klog.FromContext(ctx)
284: 	wait.UntilWithContext(ctx, func(ctx context.Context) {
285: 		err := wait.PollUntilContextTimeout(ctx, le.config.RetryPeriod, le.config.RenewDeadline, true, func(ctx context.Context) (done bool, err error) {
286: 			if !le.config.Coordinated {
287: 				return le.tryAcquireOrRenew(ctx), nil
288: 			} else {
289: 				return le.tryCoordinatedRenew(ctx), nil
290: 			}
291: 		})
292: 		le.maybeReportTransition()
293: 		desc := le.config.Lock.Describe()
294: 		if err == nil {
295: 			logger.V(5).Info("Successfully renewed lease", "lock", desc)
296: 			return
297: 		}
298: 		le.metrics.leaderOff(le.config.Name)
299: 		logger.Info("Failed to renew lease", "lock", desc, "err", err)
300: 		cancel()
301: 	}, le.config.RetryPeriod)
302: 
303: 	// if we hold the lease, give it up
304: 	if le.config.ReleaseOnCancel {
305: 		le.release(logger)
306: 	}
307: }
308: 
309: // release attempts to release the leader lease if we have acquired it.
310: func (le *LeaderElector) release(logger klog.Logger) bool {
311: 	ctx := context.Background()
312: 	timeoutCtx, timeoutCancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, le.config.RenewDeadline)
313: 	defer timeoutCancel()
314: 	// update the resourceVersion of lease
315: 	oldLeaderElectionRecord, _, err := le.config.Lock.Get(timeoutCtx)
316: 	if err != nil {
317: 		if !errors.IsNotFound(err) {
318: 			logger.Error(err, "error retrieving resource lock", "lock", le.config.Lock.Describe())
319: 			return false
320: 		}

30: // IsRetryableErrorFunc allows the client to provide its own function

33: // request: the original request sent to the server
34: // err: the server sent this error to us

37: // can be retried, otherwise it returns false.

39: // function allows us to customize the retryability aspect of each.
40: type IsRetryableErrorFunc func(request *http.Request, err error) bool
41: 
42: func (r IsRetryableErrorFunc) IsErrorRetryable(request *http.Request, err error) bool {
43: 	return r(request, err)

52: // goroutines without additional locking or coordination.

54: 	// IsNextRetry advances the retry counter appropriately

60: 	//    'Retry-After' response header is not set with a value.
61: 	//  - we need to seek to the beginning of the request body before we
62: 	//    initiate the next retry, the function should log an error and
63: 	//    return false if it fails to do so.
64: 	//
65: 	// restReq: the associated rest.Request
66: 	// httpReq: the HTTP Request sent to the server
67: 	// resp: the response sent from the server, it is set if err is nil
68: 	// err: the server sent this error to us, if err is set then resp is nil.
69: 	// f: a IsRetryableErrorFunc function provided by the client that determines
70: 	//    if the err sent by the server is retryable.
71: 	IsNextRetry(ctx context.Context, restReq *Request, httpReq *http.Request, resp *http.Response, err error, f IsRetryableErrorFunc) bool

29: // HTTPClientFor returns an http.Client that will provide the authentication
30: // or transport level security defined by the provided Config. Will return the
31: // default http.DefaultClient if no special case behavior is needed.
32: func HTTPClientFor(config *Config) (*http.Client, error) {
33: 	transport, err := TransportFor(config)
34: 	if err != nil {
35: 		return nil, err
36: 	}
37: 	var httpClient *http.Client
38: 	if transport != http.DefaultTransport || config.Timeout > 0 {
39: 		httpClient = &http.Client{
40: 			Transport: transport,
41: 			Timeout:   config.Timeout,
42: 		}
43: 	} else {
44: 		httpClient = http.DefaultClient
45: 	}
46: 
47: 	return httpClient, nil
48: }
49: 
50: // TLSConfigFor returns a tls.Config that will provide the transport level security defined
51: // by the provided Config. Will return nil if no transport level security is requested.
52: func TLSConfigFor(config *Config) (*tls.Config, error) {
53: 	cfg, err := config.TransportConfig()
54: 	if err != nil {
55: 		return nil, err
56: 	}
57: 	return transport.TLSConfigFor(cfg)
58: }
59: 
60: // TransportFor returns an http.RoundTripper that will provide the authentication
61: // or transport level security defined by the provided Config. Will return the
62: // default http.DefaultTransport if no special case behavior is needed.
63: func TransportFor(config *Config) (http.RoundTripper, error) {
64: 	cfg, err := config.TransportConfig()
65: 	if err != nil {
66: 		return nil, err
67: 	}
68: 	return transport.New(cfg)
69: }
70: 
71: // HTTPWrappersForConfig wraps a round tripper with any relevant layered behavior from the
72: // config. Exposed to allow more clients that need HTTP-like behavior but then must hijack
73: // the underlying connection (like WebSocket or HTTP2 clients). Pure HTTP clients should use
74: // the higher level TransportFor or RESTClientFor methods.
75: func HTTPWrappersForConfig(config *Config, rt http.RoundTripper) (http.RoundTripper, error) {
76: 	cfg, err := config.TransportConfig()
77: 	if err != nil {
78: 		return nil, err
79: 	}
80: 	return transport.HTTPWrappersForConfig(cfg, rt)

585: // list simply lists all items and records a resource version obtained from the server at the moment of the call.
586: // the resource version can be used for further progress notification (aka. watch).
587: func (r *Reflector) list(ctx context.Context) error {
588: 	var resourceVersion string
589: 	options := metav1.ListOptions{ResourceVersion: r.relistResourceVersion()}
590: 
591: 	initTrace := trace.New("Reflector ListAndWatch", trace.Field{Key: "name", Value: r.name})
592: 	defer initTrace.LogIfLong(10 * time.Second)
593: 	var list runtime.Object
594: 	var paginatedResult bool
595: 	var err error
596: 	listCh := make(chan struct{}, 1)
597: 	panicCh := make(chan interface{}, 1)
598: 	go func() {
599: 		defer func() {
600: 			if r := recover(); r != nil {
601: 				panicCh <- r
602: 			}
603: 		}()
604: 		// Attempt to gather list in chunks, if supported by listerWatcher, if not, the first
605: 		// list request will return the full response.
606: 		pager := pager.New(pager.SimplePageFunc(func(opts metav1.ListOptions) (runtime.Object, error) {
607: 			return r.listerWatcher.ListWithContext(ctx, opts)
608: 		}))
609: 		switch {
610: 		case r.WatchListPageSize != 0:
611: 			pager.PageSize = r.WatchListPageSize
612: 		case r.paginatedResult:
613: 			// We got a paginated result initially. Assume this resource and server honor
614: 			// paging requests (i.e. watch cache is probably disabled) and leave the default
615: 			// pager size set.
616: 		case options.ResourceVersion != "" && options.ResourceVersion != "0":
617: 			// User didn't explicitly request pagination.
618: 			//
619: 			// With ResourceVersion != "", we have a possibility to list from watch cache,
620: 			// but we do that (for ResourceVersion != "0") only if Limit is unset.
621: 			// To avoid thundering herd on etcd (e.g. on master upgrades), we explicitly
622: 			// switch off pagination to force listing from watch cache (if enabled).
623: 			// With the existing semantic of RV (result is at least as fresh as provided RV),
624: 			// this is correct and doesn't lead to going back in time.
625: 			//
626: 			// We also don't turn off pagination for ResourceVersion="0", since watch cache
627: 			// is ignoring Limit in that case anyway, and if watch cache is not enabled
628: 			// we don't introduce regression.
629: 			pager.PageSize = 0
630: 		}
631: 
632: 		list, paginatedResult, err = pager.ListWithAlloc(context.Background(), options)
633: 		if isExpiredError(err) || isTooLargeResourceVersionError(err) {
634: 			r.setIsLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable(true)
635: 			// Retry immediately if the resource version used to list is unavailable.
636: 			// The pager already falls back to full list if paginated list calls fail due to an "Expired" error on
637: 			// continuation pages, but the pager might not be enabled, the full list might fail because the
638: 			// resource version it is listing at is expired or the cache may not yet be synced to the provided
639: 			// resource version. So we need to fallback to resourceVersion="" in all to recover and ensure
640: 			// the reflector makes forward progress.
641: 			list, paginatedResult, err = pager.ListWithAlloc(context.Background(), metav1.ListOptions{ResourceVersion: r.relistResourceVersion()})
642: 		}
643: 		close(listCh)
644: 	}()
645: 	select {
646: 	case <-ctx.Done():
647: 		return nil
648: 	case r := <-panicCh:
649: 		panic(r)
650: 	case <-listCh:
651: 	}
652: 	initTrace.Step("Objects listed", trace.Field{Key: "error", Value: err})
653: 	if err != nil {
654: 		return fmt.Errorf("failed to list %v: %w", r.typeDescription, err)
655: 	}
656: 
657: 	// We check if the list was paginated and if so set the paginatedResult based on that.
658: 	// However, we want to do that only for the initial list (which is the only case
659: 	// when we set ResourceVersion="0"). The reasoning behind it is that later, in some
660: 	// situations we may force listing directly from etcd (by setting ResourceVersion="")
661: 	// which will return paginated result, even if watch cache is enabled. However, in
662: 	// that case, we still want to prefer sending requests to watch cache if possible.
663: 	//
664: 	// Paginated result returned for request with ResourceVersion="0" mean that watch
665: 	// cache is disabled and there are a lot of objects of a given type. In such case,
666: 	// there is no need to prefer listing from watch cache.
667: 	if options.ResourceVersion == "0" && paginatedResult {
668: 		r.paginatedResult = true
669: 	}
670: 
671: 	r.setIsLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable(false) // list was successful
672: 	listMetaInterface, err := meta.ListAccessor(list)
673: 	if err != nil {

675: 	}
676: 	resourceVersion = listMetaInterface.GetResourceVersion()
677: 	initTrace.Step("Resource version extracted")
678: 	items, err := meta.ExtractListWithAlloc(list)
679: 	if err != nil {
680: 		return fmt.Errorf("unable to understand list result %#v (%v)", list, err)
681: 	}
682: 	initTrace.Step("Objects extracted")
683: 	if err := r.syncWith(items, resourceVersion); err != nil {
684: 		return fmt.Errorf("unable to sync list result: %v", err)
685: 	}
686: 	initTrace.Step("SyncWith done")
687: 	r.setLastSyncResourceVersion(resourceVersion)
688: 	initTrace.Step("Resource version updated")
689: 	return nil
690: }

36: // HTTPWrappersForConfig wraps a round tripper with any relevant layered
37: // behavior from the config. Exposed to allow more clients that need HTTP-like
38: // behavior but then must hijack the underlying connection (like WebSocket or
39: // HTTP2 clients). Pure HTTP clients should use the RoundTripper returned from
40: // New.
41: func HTTPWrappersForConfig(config *Config, rt http.RoundTripper) (http.RoundTripper, error) {
42: 	if config.WrapTransport != nil {
43: 		rt = config.WrapTransport(rt)
44: 	}
45: 
46: 	rt = DebugWrappers(rt)
47: 
48: 	// Set authentication wrappers
49: 	switch {
50: 	case config.HasBasicAuth() && config.HasTokenAuth():
51: 		return nil, fmt.Errorf("username/password or bearer token may be set, but not both")
52: 	case config.HasTokenAuth():
53: 		var err error
54: 		rt, err = NewBearerAuthWithRefreshRoundTripper(config.BearerToken, config.BearerTokenFile, rt)
55: 		if err != nil {
56: 			return nil, err
57: 		}
58: 	case config.HasBasicAuth():
59: 		rt = NewBasicAuthRoundTripper(config.Username, config.Password, rt)
60: 	}
61: 	if len(config.UserAgent) > 0 {
62: 		rt = NewUserAgentRoundTripper(config.UserAgent, rt)
63: 	}
64: 	if len(config.Impersonate.UserName) > 0 ||
65: 		len(config.Impersonate.UID) > 0 ||
66: 		len(config.Impersonate.Groups) > 0 ||
67: 		len(config.Impersonate.Extra) > 0 {
68: 		rt = NewImpersonatingRoundTripper(config.Impersonate, rt)
69: 	}
70: 	return rt, nil
71: }

74: // based on the log level in the context of each individual request.

77: // if that verbosity is >= 6. This may change in the future.

80: 	if klog.V(6).Enabled() {
81: 		rt = NewDebuggingRoundTripper(rt, DebugByContext)

# client-go: Advanced Systems Guide

> Repository snapshot: `github.com/sg-benchmarks/kubernetes--stripped`
> Primary tree: `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/`

---

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

`client-go` is the canonical Go client library for the Kubernetes API. It serves two distinct audiences: the internal control-plane components (kubelet, kube-controller-manager, scheduler) and external operators and controllers written by third parties. It is not a thin HTTP wrapper; it contains a complete, self-consistent framework for building event-driven controllers that are resilient to API-server restarts, network partitions, and resource-version drift.

### What client-go owns

- **HTTP transport assembly** — TLS, authentication, rate limiting, retry, and content negotiation.
- **Typed and untyped client models** — `kubernetes.Clientset` and `dynamic.DynamicClient`.
- **Controller infrastructure** — `Reflector`, `DeltaFIFO`, `Indexer`, `SharedIndexInformer`, `sharedProcessor`, and `workqueue`.
- **High-level patterns** — `SharedInformerFactory`, leader election, Server-Side Apply builders, event recording.
- **Code-generation output** — typed listers, typed informers, and apply-configuration builders.

### What client-go does NOT own

| Concern | Owner |
|---------|-------|
| API object schemas (Pod, Deployment, …) | `k8s.io/api` |
| Runtime serialization (JSON, Protobuf, CBOR) | `k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime` |
| `kubeconfig` file format | `k8s.io/client-go/tools/clientcmd` (within the library, but logically separate) |
| API Priority and Fairness server logic | `k8s.io/apiserver` |
| Code generation tools | `k8s.io/code-generator` |

### Upstream dependencies (what client-go consumes)

- `k8s.io/apimachinery` — object meta, watch events, list/get options, runtime serializer interfaces.
- `k8s.io/api` — concrete API type structs that typed clients encode/decode.
- `golang.org/x/time/rate` — token-bucket implementation used by the workqueue rate limiter.
- `k8s.io/utils/clock` — injectable clock for testing time-dependent code.

### Downstream dependents (what consumes client-go)

- Every Kubernetes control-plane component (`kube-controller-manager`, `kubelet`, `cloud-controller-manager`).
- All community and vendor operators, typically via the higher-level `controller-runtime` framework.
- `kubectl` (via the `dynamic` client and `tools/clientcmd`).

### Versioning contract

`client-go` version `v0.X.Y` aligns with Kubernetes `v1.X.Y`. An older client works against a newer API server (n-3 skew), but not the reverse. This guarantee shapes the entire compatibility surface of the library.
(`staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/ARCHITECTURE.md`, lines 155–162)

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

### 2.1 Configuration Layer

**`rest.Config`** (`rest/config.go:55`) is the in-memory configuration object. It carries:

- Target host and API path.
- Authentication credentials: `BearerToken`, `BearerTokenFile` (live-reloaded), `Username`/`Password`, TLS client cert, `ExecProvider` (exec-based credential plugins), `AuthProvider` (legacy plugin interface).
- Transport customization: `WrapTransport`, `Transport`, `Dial`, `Proxy`.
- Client-side rate limiting: `QPS` (default 5.0), `Burst` (default 10).
- Content negotiation: `ContentType`, `AcceptContentTypes`.

Two canonical factories produce a `Config`:

- `InClusterConfig()` (`rest/config.go:543`) — reads the pod service-account token from `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token` and CA from `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt`.
- `tools/clientcmd` — merges `kubeconfig` files, resolves contexts, and handles exec-based auth.

`Config.String()` and `Config.GoString()` redact sensitive fields (`BearerToken`, `Password`, `KeyData`) so that the struct can safely be logged (`rest/config.go:192`).

### 2.2 Transport Layer

`HTTPClientFor(config)` → `TransportFor(config)` → `transport.New(cfg)` (`rest/transport.go:32`).

`transport.HTTPWrappersForConfig` (`transport/round_trippers.go:41`) assembles a chain of `http.RoundTripper` decorators in a fixed order:

1. **Custom `WrapTransport`** — caller-injected.
2. **`DebugWrappers`** — activated when `klog` verbosity ≥ 6; emits per-request HTTP traces.
3. **Auth wrapper** — exactly one of: `BearerAuthWithRefreshRoundTripper` (reads the token file on each request), `BasicAuthRoundTripper`, or nothing.
4. **`UserAgentRoundTripper`** — appends `User-Agent: <binary>/<version> (<os>/<arch>) kubernetes/<commit>`.
5. **`ImpersonatingRoundTripper`** — injects `Impersonate-User`, `Impersonate-Group`, and `Impersonate-Extra-*` headers.

The outer `http.Client` has a configurable `Timeout` and shares a connection pool per `Config.Host`. Because the auth wrapper reads `BearerTokenFile` on every call, credential rotation happens transparently without restarting the client.

### 2.3 REST Client

**`RESTClient`** (`rest/client.go:86`) is the foundational HTTP client. It exposes a builder-pattern request API (`Verb()`, `Resource()`, `Namespace()`, `Name()`, `Body()`, `Do()`, `Watch()`, `Stream()`).

The **`Request`** struct (`rest/request.go:96`) accumulates path segments, query parameters, headers, and a body, then dispatches the assembled HTTP request through the shared `http.Client`. Key behaviors:

- **Rate limiting** — each `Request` holds a reference to the shared `flowcontrol.RateLimiter`. `Request.Do()` calls `rateLimiter.Wait()` before sending, throttling bursts against the configured QPS. Throttle events exceeding `longThrottleLatency` (50ms) are logged at v4; those exceeding `extraLongThrottleLatency` (1s) at v2 (`rest/request.go:54`).
- **Retry on 429/5xx** — the `WithRetry` interface (`rest/with_retry.go:53`) controls retry behavior. The default `withRetry` implementation reads the `Retry-After` header from `429 Too Many Requests` responses and backs off accordingly. This is the client-side complement to API Priority and Fairness.
- **Content negotiation** — the `Accept` header is set from `AcceptContentTypes` or `ContentType`. Controllers that only need metadata can request `as=PartialObjectMetadata;g=meta.k8s.io;v=v1` to reduce payload size.
- **Watch** — `Request.Watch()` returns a `watch.Interface` backed by a chunked HTTP response decoder (`rest/watch/`). Each decoded chunk is emitted as a `watch.Event` with type `ADDED`, `MODIFIED`, `DELETED`, or `BOOKMARK`.

### 2.4 Typed Clientset

**`kubernetes.Clientset`** (`kubernetes/`) is a code-generated struct that composes one typed client per API group version. Every typed client is a thin adapter that calls `RESTClientFor` with a fixed `GroupVersion` and delegates to the `Request` builder. This provides compile-time type safety: errors from wrong resource kinds surface at build time, not runtime.

### 2.5 Dynamic Client

**`dynamic.DynamicClient`** (`dynamic/simple.go:34`) wraps a `rest.Interface` and works with `unstructured.Unstructured` maps. It is schema-agnostic and can interact with any API group, including CRDs, without generated code. The tradeoff is that all field access is string-keyed, losing compile-time safety.

`ConfigFor` (`dynamic/simple.go:42`) forcibly sets `ContentType` to `application/json` (or optionally `application/cbor` when the `ClientsPreferCBOR` feature gate is enabled), because the dynamic client has no registered serializer for protobuf.

### 2.6 Controller Infrastructure (`tools/cache`)

This package contains the building blocks of every event-driven controller in Kubernetes.

#### `ListerWatcher` and `ListWatch`

`ListWatch` (`tools/cache/listwatch.go:193`) implements `ListerWatcherWithContext` by composing a list function and a watch function. `NewFilteredListWatchFromClient` (`tools/cache/listwatch.go:229`) constructs both from a `Getter` (the REST client) with an optional field/label selector modifier. The list function uses `pager.New` internally through the `Reflector`, so direct callers of `ListWatch.List()` receive unpaginated results.

#### `Reflector`

**`Reflector`** (`tools/cache/reflector.go:87`) is the bridge between the Kubernetes API server and the in-process store. Its single exported behavior is expressed through `RunWithContext`, which calls `ListAndWatchWithContext` in a loop, managed by an exponential backoff manager (`800ms` base, `30s` cap, resetting after `2min` of stability).

`ListAndWatchWithContext` (`tools/cache/reflector.go:408`) does one of two things depending on the `WatchListClient` feature gate:

- **Classic mode** (`fallbackToList = true`): calls `r.list()`, which uses `pager.ListWithAlloc` to retrieve all objects in chunks. The returned `resourceVersion` is stored and used to open a `WATCH` from that exact point.
- **WatchList mode** (`useWatchList = true`): opens a single streaming watch with `ResourceVersionMatch=NotOlderThan`, receiving synthetic `ADDED` events followed by a terminal `BOOKMARK` that signals sync completion. This consumes significantly fewer server resources than paginated LIST.

The `watch` inner loop (`tools/cache/reflector.go:499`) runs until the context is canceled or a non-retriable error occurs. For each received `watch.Event` it calls `handleWatch`, which routes the event into the `ReflectorStore`. `BOOKMARK` events update `lastSyncResourceVersion` without touching the store.

Watch timeout randomization (`minWatchTimeout` to `2×minWatchTimeout`, default 5-10 minutes) spreads reconnection load across the API server's watch cache.

#### `DeltaFIFO`

**`DeltaFIFO`** (`tools/cache/delta_fifo.go:63`) is the producer-consumer queue between the Reflector and the rest of the informer machinery. Its key design property: it accumulates *all* deltas for a given object key, not just the latest state. When the consumer calls `Pop()`, it receives the full slice of deltas that arrived since the last pop. This means a controller that processes slowly will see every intermediate state batched together rather than losing events.

Delta types: `Added`, `Updated`, `Deleted`, `Replaced` (from LIST/Replace calls), `Sync` (from periodic resync). `KnownObjects` links the FIFO to the downstream `Indexer` so that `Replace()` can synthesize `Deleted` deltas for objects missing from the new list.

#### `Indexer` and `ThreadSafeStore`

**`Indexer`** (`tools/cache/index.go:35`) extends `Store` with secondary index support. The concrete implementation (`threadSafeMap` inside `tools/cache/thread_safe_store.go`) holds a map from key to object and a map of named indices. Each index is an `IndexFunc` (`tools/cache/index.go:58`) that returns a set of string-valued index keys for a given object. The built-in `MetaNamespaceIndexFunc` indexes by namespace.

`ByIndex(indexName, indexedValue)` performs a reverse lookup: given an index name and a specific value (e.g., a namespace string), it returns all matching objects. This is the mechanism that makes operations like "list all pods in namespace X" O(1) against the local cache instead of requiring a full scan.

#### `SharedIndexInformer` and `sharedProcessor`

**`sharedIndexInformer`** (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:415`) combines an `Indexer`, an internal `Controller` (which runs the `Reflector` and pops the `DeltaFIFO`), and a `sharedProcessor`. It is the main informer type used in production.

`RunWithContext` (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:529`) does:
1. Constructs a `DeltaFIFO` keyed to the `Indexer`.
2. Builds a `Config` with `HandleDeltas` as the `ProcessFunc`.
3. Creates and runs the internal `Controller`.
4. Concurrently runs the `sharedProcessor` and `CacheMutationDetector`.

`HandleDeltas` is the critical function that holds the `DeltaFIFO` lock while it:
1. Updates the `Indexer` for each delta.
2. Enqueues the corresponding notification (add/update/delete) into the `sharedProcessor`.

The **`sharedProcessor`** fans out notifications to all registered `processorListener`s. Each listener has a ring buffer (`utils/buffer`) that decouples notification production from handler execution. This means a slow handler does not block the watch loop, but will accumulate notifications in its buffer. If the buffer fills, the listener blocks, which eventually blocks the `DeltaFIFO` pop loop.

**`SharedInformerFactory`** (`informers/factory.go:57`) is the standard entry point for production controllers. It acts as a registry of `SharedIndexInformer` instances keyed by `reflect.Type`, ensuring that a single informer (and therefore a single LIST/WATCH connection) is shared across multiple controllers watching the same resource type.

### 2.7 Workqueue

**`util/workqueue`** provides three layers:

- `TypedInterface[T]` (`util/workqueue/queue.go:30`) — a basic FIFO with deduplication: adding an already-queued key is a no-op, and adding a key that is currently being processed queues it for re-processing after `Done()` is called.
- `TypedDelayingInterface[T]` — adds `AddAfter(item, delay)` for scheduled re-queuing (used for exponential backoff).
- `TypedRateLimitingInterface[T]` — wraps the delaying queue with a `TypedRateLimiter`.

**`DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter`** (`util/workqueue/default_rate_limiters.go:50`) composes two limiters with `MaxOf`:
- `TypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter` — per-item exponential backoff starting at 5ms, capped at 1000s. This is the per-item retry backoff seen in controller reconcile loops.
- `TypedBucketRateLimiter` — global token bucket at 10 QPS with burst 100. This prevents a flood of fast-failing items from hammering the API server.

### 2.8 Leader Election

**`tools/leaderelection`** provides distributed leader election via a Kubernetes `Lease` resource (or `ConfigMap`/`Endpoints` for backward compatibility). The `LeaderElector.Run` lifecycle:

1. **Acquire** — calls `tryAcquireOrRenew` in a jittered loop at `RetryPeriod` intervals until the `Lease` is acquired or the context is canceled.
2. **Lead** — launches `OnStartedLeading(ctx)` in a goroutine, then calls `renew` in a tight loop.
3. **Renew** — calls `tryAcquireOrRenew` every `RetryPeriod` within a `RenewDeadline` window. Failure to renew within the deadline cancels the leading context, triggering cleanup.
4. **Stop** — always calls `OnStoppedLeading()`.

The implementation explicitly does not guarantee a single writer (no fencing tokens), relying instead on API server optimistic concurrency (`resourceVersion` conflict detection) and bounded lease duration to limit dual-leader windows. The `LeaseDuration`:`RenewDeadline` ratio controls tolerance to clock skew.

### 2.9 Server-Side Apply Builders

**`applyconfigurations/`** contains generated structs that represent partial, nullable views of API objects for use with `PATCH application/apply-patch+yaml`. Unlike full object structs, apply-configuration structs omit unset fields entirely rather than serializing zero values, preventing accidental ownership of fields the controller doesn't manage. These are constructed with a fluent builder API and passed to a typed client's `Apply()` method.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

### 3.1 Simple CRUD Request

```
User code
  → kubernetes.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods("ns")
      → typed client calls RESTClient.Get().Namespace("ns").Resource("pods").Name("my-pod")
          → Request.Do(ctx)
              → rateLimiter.Wait(ctx)           [client-side token bucket]
              → http.Client.Do(assembledReq)    [transport chain: debug → auth → impersonate]
              → response body decoded via NegotiatedSerializer
              → on non-2xx: deserialize into errors.StatusError
              → on 429/5xx: withRetry.IsNextRetry() → backoff → retry
```

The `GroupVersion` embedded in each typed client's `RESTClient` sets the `Accept` header automatically to the correct API version. The client never needs to know the resource's URL structure beyond `Resource()`, `Namespace()`, and `Name()`.

### 3.2 Informer Startup and Watch Loop

```
SharedInformerFactory.Start(stopCh)
  → for each registered SharedIndexInformer, goroutine: informer.RunWithContext(ctx)

sharedIndexInformer.RunWithContext(ctx)
  ├─ goroutine: sharedProcessor.run(processorCtx)       [fan-out to handlers]
  ├─ goroutine: cacheMutationDetector.Run(processorCtx) [debug mode only]
  └─ controller.RunWithContext(ctx)
       ├─ goroutine: Reflector.RunWithContext(ctx)
       │    └─ loop: ListAndWatchWithContext(ctx)
       │         ├─ list(): pager.ListWithAlloc → DeltaFIFO.Replace()
       │         │       [populates Indexer; emits Replaced/Sync deltas]
       │         └─ watch(): listerWatcher.WatchWithContext → handleWatch
       │              [per event: DeltaFIFO.Add/Update/Delete]
       └─ loop: DeltaFIFO.Pop(HandleDeltas)
            HandleDeltas(deltas):
              ├─ Indexer.Add/Update/Delete          [updates in-process cache]
              └─ sharedProcessor.distribute(notif)  [enqueues to each listener's buffer]

processorListener.run() goroutine (one per handler):
  └─ loop: dequeue from ring buffer → handler.OnAdd / OnUpdate / OnDelete

User controller event handler (example):
  └─ OnAdd(obj) { workqueue.Add(keyOf(obj)) }

Controller reconcile goroutine (user code):
  └─ loop: workqueue.Get() → reconcile(key) → workqueue.Done(key)
       reconcile(key):
         ├─ lister.Pods("ns").Get(name)   [reads Indexer, no API call]
         └─ typed client.Pods("ns").Update(...)  [API call only on mutation]
```

The critical design insight: the `workqueue` decouples event arrival from reconciliation. Events arrive at watch speed; reconciliation happens at the controller's pace with built-in deduplication and retry backoff.

### 3.3 Resync

Resync does not contact the API server. The `Reflector.startResync` goroutine fires the `ShouldResync` callback periodically. When it returns true, `DeltaFIFO.Resync()` iterates the `Indexer`'s keys and enqueues a `Sync` delta for each. This causes `HandleDeltas` to re-invoke the event handler's `OnUpdate` with the current cached object, giving the controller a chance to reconcile state it may have missed.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 `resourceVersion` Staleness

The Reflector tracks `lastSyncResourceVersion`. If the API server's watch cache does not retain history back to that version (defaulting to 5 minutes of history), the watch returns a `410 Gone` error. The Reflector handles this by calling `setIsLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable(true)` and re-listing with `ResourceVersion=""` (latest) rather than the stale version, accepting a momentary full scan of etcd or the watch cache (`tools/cache/reflector.go:633`).

**Operational implication**: during control-plane upgrades or API server restarts, every informer will relist. At large scale, concurrent relisters from many controllers can create a "thundering herd" against etcd. The watch cache mitigates this, but clusters with the watch cache disabled face significant load spikes.

### 4.2 Slow Event Handlers and Back-pressure

The `sharedProcessor`'s per-listener ring buffer (`utils/buffer`) is pre-allocated at `initialBufferSize = 1024` events (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:340`). When a handler is slow, the buffer fills. Once full, `sharedProcessor.distribute()` blocks, which blocks `HandleDeltas`, which blocks `DeltaFIFO.Pop()`, which eventually stalls the `Reflector`'s event consumption. A stalled watch connection can be closed by the API server's idle timeout.

The comment in the `SharedInformer` interface documentation is explicit:
> "A client must process each notification promptly; a SharedInformer is not engineered to deal well with a large backlog of notifications." (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:130`)

This is why all production controllers hand off work to a `workqueue` in the handler rather than processing inline.

### 4.3 Client-Side Rate Limiting vs. API Priority and Fairness

The `QPS`/`Burst` token bucket in `RESTClient` is a client-side rate limiter. It protects the API server by smoothing request bursts from a single client. Kubernetes API server's API Priority and Fairness (APF) provides server-side flow control that also covers rate abuse from misconfigured clients. These two systems are complementary: the client-side limiter prevents reaching the server-side queue; APF handles cases where the client ignores its own limits or where aggregate load exceeds cluster capacity.

Setting `QPS` to -1 disables client-side limiting entirely. This is appropriate only for special clients like the controller manager's leader-election paths that must not be queued, but is dangerous in general.

### 4.4 Leader Election Gaps

The `LeaderElector` does not guarantee a single leader at any instant. If the leader process is paused (GC stop-the-world, CPU starvation, OS scheduling jitter), it may fail to renew the lease within `RenewDeadline`, causing another candidate to acquire the lease before the original leader has stopped acting. The `LeaseDuration` should be set long enough to absorb expected scheduling jitter while the `RenewDeadline`/`RetryPeriod` ratio determines recovery speed. The comment in the package documentation notes:
> "This implementation does not guarantee that only one client is acting as a leader (a.k.a. fencing)." (`tools/leaderelection/leaderelection.go:20`)

For operations that require strict single-writer semantics (e.g., writing to external systems), callers must implement their own idempotency or fencing on top of the leader election lease.

### 4.5 SharedInformerFactory and Goroutine Proliferation

Each `SharedIndexInformer` started by `SharedInformerFactory.Start()` spawns at minimum two goroutines: the Reflector loop and the `sharedProcessor`. Each registered event handler adds another goroutine (the `processorListener.run` loop). In controllers watching many resource types with many handlers, the total goroutine count grows proportionally. This is by design but must be considered during resource planning.

---

## 5. Extension Points and Customization Hooks

### 5.1 Custom `http.RoundTripper` via `WrapTransport`

`rest.Config.WrapTransport` (`rest/config.go:115`) is called after the base transport is assembled. It is the primary extension point for cross-cutting HTTP concerns: distributed tracing (injecting `traceparent` headers), custom metrics, or mutual TLS certificate rotation. The convention is to wrap rather than replace, preserving the auth and debug layers already in place.

**Risk**: A `WrapTransport` that blocks or returns errors drops all requests from the affected client. Panics inside the wrapper will propagate and crash the using goroutine.

### 5.2 `ExecProvider` for External Credential Plugins

`rest.Config.ExecProvider` (`rest/config.go:92`) delegates credential retrieval to an external binary (e.g., `aws eks get-token`). The `exec` package (`plugin/pkg/client/auth/exec/`) caches credentials and re-executes the binary when they expire. This is the standard mechanism for cloud-provider authentication without embedding credentials.

**Risk**: If the exec binary is slow or unavailable, all API calls block waiting for credentials. The exec plugin runs in a goroutine per client, so deadlines on the parent context propagate, but errors surface as generic transport errors rather than auth-specific ones.

### 5.3 `TransformFunc` on Informers

`SharedInformer.SetTransform(handler TransformFunc)` (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:231`) installs a function called on each object before it is inserted into the `Indexer`. Common uses:

- Stripping `managedFields` to reduce memory usage.
- Normalizing fields before indexing.
- Scrubbing sensitive data from the in-memory cache.

The transform runs synchronously in the `DeltaFIFO.Pop` path, under the FIFO lock. It must not access the informer's `Indexer` (deadlock risk) or perform slow I/O (back-pressure on the watch loop). Must be set before calling `Run()`.

### 5.4 Custom `Indexers`

Additional `IndexFunc` entries can be registered on a `SharedIndexInformer` via `AddIndexers()` before the informer starts (`tools/cache/index.go:58`). Custom indexes enable efficient lookups beyond namespace, such as indexing pods by node name or services by `spec.selector` labels. Each index function must be deterministic and cheap, since it runs on every object mutation.

**Risk**: Adding indexes after the informer starts returns an error. Index functions that panic will crash the goroutine processing that delta.

### 5.5 `WatchErrorHandler`

`SharedInformer.SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext()` installs a callback invoked whenever `ListAndWatch` drops its connection. The default handler logs at different levels based on error type (`io.EOF` is silent, `io.ErrUnexpectedEOF` is logged at v1, others trigger `HandleError`). A custom handler can emit metrics or alerts. The handler runs in the Reflector's goroutine context and must return quickly.

### 5.6 Custom `WorkQueue` Backend

`TypedQueueConfig.Queue` (`util/workqueue/queue.go:98`) accepts an alternative queue implementation. The default is a slice-based FIFO. Custom implementations can support priority queuing (for example, preferring recently-changed objects) by implementing `Queue[T].Touch()`, which is called when a key that is already in the queue is added again.

### 5.7 `ResourceLock` Interface for Leader Election

`tools/leaderelection/resourcelock.Interface` defines the lock abstraction used by `LeaderElector`. The standard implementations use `Lease`, `ConfigMap`, or `Endpoints` Kubernetes objects. Custom implementations could use external systems (e.g., etcd directly or a database), provided they implement optimistic concurrency semantics.

### 5.8 Server-Side Apply and Generated `applyconfigurations`

For controllers that co-manage objects with other controllers or users, Server-Side Apply via the `applyconfigurations/` builders is strongly preferred over `get-modify-update`. The controller sets only the fields it owns; the server merges with fields owned by other managers. Custom field managers can be named per-controller.

**Risk**: Using full object `Update()` in a controller that coexists with Server-Side Apply will conflict with `managedFields` ownership. Mixing paradigms on the same object causes repeated update conflicts.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### Performance

**Memory**: Each `SharedIndexInformer` holds a full in-memory copy of all objects of its type. A cluster with 100,000 pods, each 10 KB serialized, will use ~1 GB of cache per informer instance watching pods. `SetTransform` stripping `managedFields` and `status.conditions` can reduce this significantly (often 50-70% for pod objects).

**CPU**: `sharedProcessor` uses a ring buffer that avoids allocations per notification. `DeltaFIFO` uses a `sync.Cond` for producer/consumer coordination, avoiding busy-polling. The main CPU cost at steady state is JSON/protobuf decoding of watch events plus index maintenance.

**Network**: Each `SharedIndexInformer` opens one persistent HTTP/2 stream (watch connection). With HTTP/2 multiplexing, a single TCP connection can serve multiple watches. Protobuf (`application/vnd.kubernetes.protobuf`) is ~30-40% more compact than JSON and is the default for typed clients; this reduces bandwidth especially for large objects.

**Rate limiting**: The default `QPS=5, Burst=10` in `rest.Config` is appropriate for controllers doing occasional reconciliation. Controllers that list or update many objects per reconcile loop (e.g., managing 1,000 Pods per second) must raise these values, balancing against APF priority levels on the server.

### Reliability

**Watch reconnect**: The Reflector's exponential backoff (base 800ms, cap 30s, jitter) prevents stampede after an API server restart. After 2 minutes of stable operation the backoff resets, ensuring normal watch reconnection latency returns to near-zero.

**`HasSynced()`**: Controllers should gate reconciliation on `WaitForNamedCacheSync` or `WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext`. `HasSynced()` returns `true` only after the informer has completed its initial LIST. Acting before sync may cause controllers to re-create objects that already exist, generating unnecessary updates and potential conflicts.

**Handler panic recovery**: `sharedIndexInformer.RunWithContext` wraps with `utilruntime.HandleCrashWithContext`, which logs panics and continues running. However, panics inside user-supplied event handlers running in `processorListener.run` goroutines are not caught by this wrapper — they must be caught by the user, or the listener goroutine terminates silently and the handler stops receiving events.

### Maintainability

**Code generation**: The typed clients, listers, and informers in `kubernetes/`, `listers/`, and `informers/` are fully generated by `hack/update-codegen.sh`. Manual edits will be overwritten. Any modification to a built-in API type requires regenerating all three layers. Custom resource types follow the same pattern using `k8s.io/code-generator`.

**Feature gates**: Several behaviors are controlled by `k8s.io/client-go/features` gate checks at runtime. Notable gates: `WatchListClient` (streaming initialization), `ClientsAllowCBOR` / `ClientsPreferCBOR` (CBOR wire format), `InOrderInformersBatchProcess` (batch delta processing). Enabling these in production without testing in staging first can change memory and CPU behavior non-trivially.

---

## 7. Critical File Map

| Path | Role |
|------|------|
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Authoritative architectural overview from maintainers |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/config.go` | `rest.Config` struct; `InClusterConfig()`; `RESTClientFor()`; rate limiter initialization |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/client.go` | `RESTClient` struct; `NewRESTClient()`; `Interface` definition |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/request.go` | `Request` builder; rate limit enforcement; retry loop; `Do()`, `Watch()`, `Stream()` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/with_retry.go` | `WithRetry` interface; 429/5xx backoff contract |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/transport.go` | `HTTPClientFor()`; `TransportFor()`; delegates to `transport.New()` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/transport/round_trippers.go` | `HTTPWrappersForConfig()`; assembles auth/debug/user-agent/impersonation chain |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/dynamic/simple.go` | `DynamicClient`; `ConfigFor()` forcing JSON content type |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/listwatch.go` | `ListerWatcher`, `ListerWatcherWithContext` interfaces; `ListWatch`; `NewFilteredListWatchFromClient()` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/reflector.go` | `Reflector`; `ListAndWatchWithContext()`; `list()`; `watchList()`; `watch()` loop; backoff manager |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/delta_fifo.go` | `DeltaFIFO`; delta accumulation semantics; `Replace()` logic for resync |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/store.go` | `Store` interface; `TransactionStore`; `KeyFunc` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/index.go` | `Indexer` interface; `IndexFunc`; `MetaNamespaceIndexFunc` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/controller.go` | Low-level `Controller` and `Config`; `ProcessFunc`; bridges Reflector to `DeltaFIFO.Pop` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/shared_informer.go` | `SharedInformer`/`SharedIndexInformer` interfaces; `sharedIndexInformer`; `sharedProcessor`; `HandleDeltas`; `WaitForCacheSync` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/informers/factory.go` | `SharedInformerFactory`; per-type informer registry; `WithTransform`/`WithTweakListOptions` options |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/queue.go` | `TypedInterface`; deduplication semantics; `TypedQueueConfig` |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/default_rate_limiters.go` | `DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter()`; per-item exponential + global token bucket |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/util/workqueue/delaying_queue.go` | `AddAfter()` for scheduled re-queuing |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/leaderelection/leaderelection.go` | `LeaderElector`; `LeaderElectionConfig`; `acquire()`/`renew()` lifecycle |

| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/applyconfigurations/` | Generated Server-Side Apply builder structs per API type |

# client-go: Advanced Systems Guide

> Repository snapshot: `github.com/sg-benchmarks/kubernetes--stripped`
> Primary tree: `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/`

---

## 1. Subsystem Purpose, Boundaries, and Dependencies

`client-go` is the canonical Go client library for the Kubernetes API. It serves two distinct audiences: the internal control-plane components (kubelet, kube-controller-manager, scheduler) and external operators and controllers written by third parties. It is not a thin HTTP wrapper; it contains a complete, self-consistent framework for building event-driven controllers that are resilient to API-server restarts, network partitions, and resource-version drift.

### What client-go owns

- **HTTP transport assembly** — TLS, authentication, rate limiting, retry, and content negotiation.
- **Typed and untyped client models** — `kubernetes.Clientset` and `dynamic.DynamicClient`.
- **Controller infrastructure** — `Reflector`, `DeltaFIFO`, `Indexer`, `SharedIndexInformer`, `sharedProcessor`, and `workqueue`.
- **High-level patterns** — `SharedInformerFactory`, leader election, Server-Side Apply builders, event recording.
- **Code-generation output** — typed listers, typed informers, and apply-configuration builders.

### What client-go does NOT own

| Concern | Owner |
|---------|-------|
| API object schemas (Pod, Deployment, …) | `k8s.io/api` |
| Runtime serialization (JSON, Protobuf, CBOR) | `k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime` |
| `kubeconfig` file format | `k8s.io/client-go/tools/clientcmd` (within the library, but logically separate) |
| API Priority and Fairness server logic | `k8s.io/apiserver` |
| Code generation tools | `k8s.io/code-generator` |

### Upstream dependencies (what client-go consumes)

- `k8s.io/apimachinery` — object meta, watch events, list/get options, runtime serializer interfaces.
- `k8s.io/api` — concrete API type structs that typed clients encode/decode.
- `golang.org/x/time/rate` — token-bucket implementation used by the workqueue rate limiter.
- `k8s.io/utils/clock` — injectable clock for testing time-dependent code.

### Downstream dependents (what consumes client-go)

- Every Kubernetes control-plane component (`kube-controller-manager`, `kubelet`, `cloud-controller-manager`).
- All community and vendor operators, typically via the higher-level `controller-runtime` framework.
- `kubectl` (via the `dynamic` client and `tools/clientcmd`).

### Versioning contract

`client-go` version `v0.X.Y` aligns with Kubernetes `v1.X.Y`. An older client works against a newer API server (n-3 skew), but not the reverse. This guarantee shapes the entire compatibility surface of the library.
(`staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/ARCHITECTURE.md`, lines 155–162)

---

## 2. Key Components and Responsibility Split

### 2.1 Configuration Layer

**`rest.Config`** (`rest/config.go:55`) is the in-memory configuration object. It carries:

- Target host and API path.
- Authentication credentials: `BearerToken`, `BearerTokenFile` (live-reloaded), `Username`/`Password`, TLS client cert, `ExecProvider` (exec-based credential plugins), `AuthProvider` (legacy plugin interface).
- Transport customization: `WrapTransport`, `Transport`, `Dial`, `Proxy`.
- Client-side rate limiting: `QPS` (default 5.0), `Burst` (default 10).
- Content negotiation: `ContentType`, `AcceptContentTypes`.

Two canonical factories produce a `Config`:

- `InClusterConfig()` (`rest/config.go:543`) — reads the pod service-account token from `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token` and CA from `/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt`.
- `tools/clientcmd` — merges `kubeconfig` files, resolves contexts, and handles exec-based auth.

`Config.String()` and `Config.GoString()` redact sensitive fields (`BearerToken`, `Password`, `KeyData`) so that the struct can safely be logged (`rest/config.go:192`).

### 2.2 Transport Layer

`HTTPClientFor(config)` → `TransportFor(config)` → `transport.New(cfg)` (`rest/transport.go:32`).

`transport.HTTPWrappersForConfig` (`transport/round_trippers.go:41`) assembles a chain of `http.RoundTripper` decorators in a fixed order:

1. **Custom `WrapTransport`** — caller-injected.
2. **`DebugWrappers`** — activated when `klog` verbosity ≥ 6; emits per-request HTTP traces.
3. **Auth wrapper** — exactly one of: `BearerAuthWithRefreshRoundTripper` (reads the token file on each request), `BasicAuthRoundTripper`, or nothing.
4. **`UserAgentRoundTripper`** — appends `User-Agent: <binary>/<version> (<os>/<arch>) kubernetes/<commit>`.
5. **`ImpersonatingRoundTripper`** — injects `Impersonate-User`, `Impersonate-Group`, and `Impersonate-Extra-*` headers.

The outer `http.Client` has a configurable `Timeout` and shares a connection pool per `Config.Host`. Because the auth wrapper reads `BearerTokenFile` on every call, credential rotation happens transparently without restarting the client.

### 2.3 REST Client

**`RESTClient`** (`rest/client.go:86`) is the foundational HTTP client. It exposes a builder-pattern request API (`Verb()`, `Resource()`, `Namespace()`, `Name()`, `Body()`, `Do()`, `Watch()`, `Stream()`).

The **`Request`** struct (`rest/request.go:96`) accumulates path segments, query parameters, headers, and a body, then dispatches the assembled HTTP request through the shared `http.Client`. Key behaviors:

- **Rate limiting** — each `Request` holds a reference to the shared `flowcontrol.RateLimiter`. `Request.Do()` calls `rateLimiter.Wait()` before sending, throttling bursts against the configured QPS. Throttle events exceeding `longThrottleLatency` (50ms) are logged at v4; those exceeding `extraLongThrottleLatency` (1s) at v2 (`rest/request.go:54`).
- **Retry on 429/5xx** — the `WithRetry` interface (`rest/with_retry.go:53`) controls retry behavior. The default `withRetry` implementation reads the `Retry-After` header from `429 Too Many Requests` responses and backs off accordingly. This is the client-side complement to API Priority and Fairness.
- **Content negotiation** — the `Accept` header is set from `AcceptContentTypes` or `ContentType`. Controllers that only need metadata can request `as=PartialObjectMetadata;g=meta.k8s.io;v=v1` to reduce payload size.
- **Watch** — `Request.Watch()` returns a `watch.Interface` backed by a chunked HTTP response decoder (`rest/watch/`). Each decoded chunk is emitted as a `watch.Event` with type `ADDED`, `MODIFIED`, `DELETED`, or `BOOKMARK`.

### 2.4 Typed Clientset

**`kubernetes.Clientset`** (`kubernetes/`) is a code-generated struct that composes one typed client per API group version. Every typed client is a thin adapter that calls `RESTClientFor` with a fixed `GroupVersion` and delegates to the `Request` builder. This provides compile-time type safety: errors from wrong resource kinds surface at build time, not runtime.

### 2.5 Dynamic Client

**`dynamic.DynamicClient`** (`dynamic/simple.go:34`) wraps a `rest.Interface` and works with `unstructured.Unstructured` maps. It is schema-agnostic and can interact with any API group, including CRDs, without generated code. The tradeoff is that all field access is string-keyed, losing compile-time safety.

`ConfigFor` (`dynamic/simple.go:42`) forcibly sets `ContentType` to `application/json` (or optionally `application/cbor` when the `ClientsPreferCBOR` feature gate is enabled), because the dynamic client has no registered serializer for protobuf.

### 2.6 Controller Infrastructure (`tools/cache`)

This package contains the building blocks of every event-driven controller in Kubernetes.

#### `ListerWatcher` and `ListWatch`

`ListWatch` (`tools/cache/listwatch.go:193`) implements `ListerWatcherWithContext` by composing a list function and a watch function. `NewFilteredListWatchFromClient` (`tools/cache/listwatch.go:229`) constructs both from a `Getter` (the REST client) with an optional field/label selector modifier. The list function uses `pager.New` internally through the `Reflector`, so direct callers of `ListWatch.List()` receive unpaginated results.

#### `Reflector`

**`Reflector`** (`tools/cache/reflector.go:87`) is the bridge between the Kubernetes API server and the in-process store. Its single exported behavior is expressed through `RunWithContext`, which calls `ListAndWatchWithContext` in a loop, managed by an exponential backoff manager (`800ms` base, `30s` cap, resetting after `2min` of stability).

`ListAndWatchWithContext` (`tools/cache/reflector.go:408`) does one of two things depending on the `WatchListClient` feature gate:

- **Classic mode** (`fallbackToList = true`): calls `r.list()`, which uses `pager.ListWithAlloc` to retrieve all objects in chunks. The returned `resourceVersion` is stored and used to open a `WATCH` from that exact point.
- **WatchList mode** (`useWatchList = true`): opens a single streaming watch with `ResourceVersionMatch=NotOlderThan`, receiving synthetic `ADDED` events followed by a terminal `BOOKMARK` that signals sync completion. This consumes significantly fewer server resources than paginated LIST.

The `watch` inner loop (`tools/cache/reflector.go:499`) runs until the context is canceled or a non-retriable error occurs. For each received `watch.Event` it calls `handleWatch`, which routes the event into the `ReflectorStore`. `BOOKMARK` events update `lastSyncResourceVersion` without touching the store.

Watch timeout randomization (`minWatchTimeout` to `2×minWatchTimeout`, default 5-10 minutes) spreads reconnection load across the API server's watch cache.

#### `DeltaFIFO`

**`DeltaFIFO`** (`tools/cache/delta_fifo.go:63`) is the producer-consumer queue between the Reflector and the rest of the informer machinery. Its key design property: it accumulates *all* deltas for a given object key, not just the latest state. When the consumer calls `Pop()`, it receives the full slice of deltas that arrived since the last pop. This means a controller that processes slowly will see every intermediate state batched together rather than losing events.

Delta types: `Added`, `Updated`, `Deleted`, `Replaced` (from LIST/Replace calls), `Sync` (from periodic resync). `KnownObjects` links the FIFO to the downstream `Indexer` so that `Replace()` can synthesize `Deleted` deltas for objects missing from the new list.

#### `Indexer` and `ThreadSafeStore`

**`Indexer`** (`tools/cache/index.go:35`) extends `Store` with secondary index support. The concrete implementation (`threadSafeMap` inside `tools/cache/thread_safe_store.go`) holds a map from key to object and a map of named indices. Each index is an `IndexFunc` (`tools/cache/index.go:58`) that returns a set of string-valued index keys for a given object. The built-in `MetaNamespaceIndexFunc` indexes by namespace.

`ByIndex(indexName, indexedValue)` performs a reverse lookup: given an index name and a specific value (e.g., a namespace string), it returns all matching objects. This is the mechanism that makes operations like "list all pods in namespace X" O(1) against the local cache instead of requiring a full scan.

#### `SharedIndexInformer` and `sharedProcessor`

**`sharedIndexInformer`** (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:415`) combines an `Indexer`, an internal `Controller` (which runs the `Reflector` and pops the `DeltaFIFO`), and a `sharedProcessor`. It is the main informer type used in production.

`RunWithContext` (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:529`) does:
1. Constructs a `DeltaFIFO` keyed to the `Indexer`.
2. Builds a `Config` with `HandleDeltas` as the `ProcessFunc`.
3. Creates and runs the internal `Controller`.
4. Concurrently runs the `sharedProcessor` and `CacheMutationDetector`.

`HandleDeltas` is the critical function that holds the `DeltaFIFO` lock while it:
1. Updates the `Indexer` for each delta.
2. Enqueues the corresponding notification (add/update/delete) into the `sharedProcessor`.

The **`sharedProcessor`** fans out notifications to all registered `processorListener`s. Each listener has a ring buffer (`utils/buffer`) that decouples notification production from handler execution. This means a slow handler does not block the watch loop, but will accumulate notifications in its buffer. If the buffer fills, the listener blocks, which eventually blocks the `DeltaFIFO` pop loop.

**`SharedInformerFactory`** (`informers/factory.go:57`) is the standard entry point for production controllers. It acts as a registry of `SharedIndexInformer` instances keyed by `reflect.Type`, ensuring that a single informer (and therefore a single LIST/WATCH connection) is shared across multiple controllers watching the same resource type.

### 2.7 Workqueue

**`util/workqueue`** provides three layers:

- `TypedInterface[T]` (`util/workqueue/queue.go:30`) — a basic FIFO with deduplication: adding an already-queued key is a no-op, and adding a key that is currently being processed queues it for re-processing after `Done()` is called.
- `TypedDelayingInterface[T]` — adds `AddAfter(item, delay)` for scheduled re-queuing (used for exponential backoff).
- `TypedRateLimitingInterface[T]` — wraps the delaying queue with a `TypedRateLimiter`.

**`DefaultTypedControllerRateLimiter`** (`util/workqueue/default_rate_limiters.go:50`) composes two limiters with `MaxOf`:
- `TypedItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter` — per-item exponential backoff starting at 5ms, capped at 1000s. This is the per-item retry backoff seen in controller reconcile loops.
- `TypedBucketRateLimiter` — global token bucket at 10 QPS with burst 100. This prevents a flood of fast-failing items from hammering the API server.

### 2.8 Leader Election

**`tools/leaderelection`** provides distributed leader election via a Kubernetes `Lease` resource (or `ConfigMap`/`Endpoints` for backward compatibility). The `LeaderElector.Run` lifecycle:

1. **Acquire** — calls `tryAcquireOrRenew` in a jittered loop at `RetryPeriod` intervals until the `Lease` is acquired or the context is canceled.
2. **Lead** — launches `OnStartedLeading(ctx)` in a goroutine, then calls `renew` in a tight loop.
3. **Renew** — calls `tryAcquireOrRenew` every `RetryPeriod` within a `RenewDeadline` window. Failure to renew within the deadline cancels the leading context, triggering cleanup.
4. **Stop** — always calls `OnStoppedLeading()`.

The implementation explicitly does not guarantee a single writer (no fencing tokens), relying instead on API server optimistic concurrency (`resourceVersion` conflict detection) and bounded lease duration to limit dual-leader windows. The `LeaseDuration`:`RenewDeadline` ratio controls tolerance to clock skew.

### 2.9 Server-Side Apply Builders

**`applyconfigurations/`** contains generated structs that represent partial, nullable views of API objects for use with `PATCH application/apply-patch+yaml`. Unlike full object structs, apply-configuration structs omit unset fields entirely rather than serializing zero values, preventing accidental ownership of fields the controller doesn't manage. These are constructed with a fluent builder API and passed to a typed client's `Apply()` method.

---

## 3. End-to-End Request Flow

### 3.1 Simple CRUD Request

```
User code
  → kubernetes.Clientset.CoreV1().Pods("ns")
      → typed client calls RESTClient.Get().Namespace("ns").Resource("pods").Name("my-pod")
          → Request.Do(ctx)
              → rateLimiter.Wait(ctx)           [client-side token bucket]
              → http.Client.Do(assembledReq)    [transport chain: debug → auth → impersonate]
              → response body decoded via NegotiatedSerializer
              → on non-2xx: deserialize into errors.StatusError
              → on 429/5xx: withRetry.IsNextRetry() → backoff → retry
```

The `GroupVersion` embedded in each typed client's `RESTClient` sets the `Accept` header automatically to the correct API version. The client never needs to know the resource's URL structure beyond `Resource()`, `Namespace()`, and `Name()`.

### 3.2 Informer Startup and Watch Loop

```
SharedInformerFactory.Start(stopCh)
  → for each registered SharedIndexInformer, goroutine: informer.RunWithContext(ctx)

sharedIndexInformer.RunWithContext(ctx)
  ├─ goroutine: sharedProcessor.run(processorCtx)       [fan-out to handlers]
  ├─ goroutine: cacheMutationDetector.Run(processorCtx) [debug mode only]
  └─ controller.RunWithContext(ctx)
       ├─ goroutine: Reflector.RunWithContext(ctx)
       │    └─ loop: ListAndWatchWithContext(ctx)
       │         ├─ list(): pager.ListWithAlloc → DeltaFIFO.Replace()
       │         │       [populates Indexer; emits Replaced/Sync deltas]
       │         └─ watch(): listerWatcher.WatchWithContext → handleWatch
       │              [per event: DeltaFIFO.Add/Update/Delete]
       └─ loop: DeltaFIFO.Pop(HandleDeltas)
            HandleDeltas(deltas):
              ├─ Indexer.Add/Update/Delete          [updates in-process cache]
              └─ sharedProcessor.distribute(notif)  [enqueues to each listener's buffer]

processorListener.run() goroutine (one per handler):
  └─ loop: dequeue from ring buffer → handler.OnAdd / OnUpdate / OnDelete

User controller event handler (example):
  └─ OnAdd(obj) { workqueue.Add(keyOf(obj)) }

Controller reconcile goroutine (user code):
  └─ loop: workqueue.Get() → reconcile(key) → workqueue.Done(key)
       reconcile(key):
         ├─ lister.Pods("ns").Get(name)   [reads Indexer, no API call]
         └─ typed client.Pods("ns").Update(...)  [API call only on mutation]
```

The critical design insight: the `workqueue` decouples event arrival from reconciliation. Events arrive at watch speed; reconciliation happens at the controller's pace with built-in deduplication and retry backoff.

### 3.3 Resync

Resync does not contact the API server. The `Reflector.startResync` goroutine fires the `ShouldResync` callback periodically. When it returns true, `DeltaFIFO.Resync()` iterates the `Indexer`'s keys and enqueues a `Sync` delta for each. This causes `HandleDeltas` to re-invoke the event handler's `OnUpdate` with the current cached object, giving the controller a chance to reconcile state it may have missed.

---

## 4. Failure Modes, Limits, and Design Tradeoffs

### 4.1 `resourceVersion` Staleness

The Reflector tracks `lastSyncResourceVersion`. If the API server's watch cache does not retain history back to that version (defaulting to 5 minutes of history), the watch returns a `410 Gone` error. The Reflector handles this by calling `setIsLastSyncResourceVersionUnavailable(true)` and re-listing with `ResourceVersion=""` (latest) rather than the stale version, accepting a momentary full scan of etcd or the watch cache (`tools/cache/reflector.go:633`).

**Operational implication**: during control-plane upgrades or API server restarts, every informer will relist. At large scale, concurrent relisters from many controllers can create a "thundering herd" against etcd. The watch cache mitigates this, but clusters with the watch cache disabled face significant load spikes.

### 4.2 Slow Event Handlers and Back-pressure

The `sharedProcessor`'s per-listener ring buffer (`utils/buffer`) is pre-allocated at `initialBufferSize = 1024` events (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:340`). When a handler is slow, the buffer fills. Once full, `sharedProcessor.distribute()` blocks, which blocks `HandleDeltas`, which blocks `DeltaFIFO.Pop()`, which eventually stalls the `Reflector`'s event consumption. A stalled watch connection can be closed by the API server's idle timeout.

The comment in the `SharedInformer` interface documentation is explicit:
> "A client must process each notification promptly; a SharedInformer is not engineered to deal well with a large backlog of notifications." (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:130`)

This is why all production controllers hand off work to a `workqueue` in the handler rather than processing inline.

### 4.3 Client-Side Rate Limiting vs. API Priority and Fairness

The `QPS`/`Burst` token bucket in `RESTClient` is a client-side rate limiter. It protects the API server by smoothing request bursts from a single client. Kubernetes API server's API Priority and Fairness (APF) provides server-side flow control that also covers rate abuse from misconfigured clients. These two systems are complementary: the client-side limiter prevents reaching the server-side queue; APF handles cases where the client ignores its own limits or where aggregate load exceeds cluster capacity.

Setting `QPS` to -1 disables client-side limiting entirely. This is appropriate only for special clients like the controller manager's leader-election paths that must not be queued, but is dangerous in general.

### 4.4 Leader Election Gaps

The `LeaderElector` does not guarantee a single leader at any instant. If the leader process is paused (GC stop-the-world, CPU starvation, OS scheduling jitter), it may fail to renew the lease within `RenewDeadline`, causing another candidate to acquire the lease before the original leader has stopped acting. The `LeaseDuration` should be set long enough to absorb expected scheduling jitter while the `RenewDeadline`/`RetryPeriod` ratio determines recovery speed. The comment in the package documentation notes:
> "This implementation does not guarantee that only one client is acting as a leader (a.k.a. fencing)." (`tools/leaderelection/leaderelection.go:20`)

For operations that require strict single-writer semantics (e.g., writing to external systems), callers must implement their own idempotency or fencing on top of the leader election lease.

### 4.5 SharedInformerFactory and Goroutine Proliferation

Each `SharedIndexInformer` started by `SharedInformerFactory.Start()` spawns at minimum two goroutines: the Reflector loop and the `sharedProcessor`. Each registered event handler adds another goroutine (the `processorListener.run` loop). In controllers watching many resource types with many handlers, the total goroutine count grows proportionally. This is by design but must be considered during resource planning.

---

## 5. Extension Points and Customization Hooks

### 5.1 Custom `http.RoundTripper` via `WrapTransport`

`rest.Config.WrapTransport` (`rest/config.go:115`) is called after the base transport is assembled. It is the primary extension point for cross-cutting HTTP concerns: distributed tracing (injecting `traceparent` headers), custom metrics, or mutual TLS certificate rotation. The convention is to wrap rather than replace, preserving the auth and debug layers already in place.

**Risk**: A `WrapTransport` that blocks or returns errors drops all requests from the affected client. Panics inside the wrapper will propagate and crash the using goroutine.

### 5.2 `ExecProvider` for External Credential Plugins

`rest.Config.ExecProvider` (`rest/config.go:92`) delegates credential retrieval to an external binary (e.g., `aws eks get-token`). The `exec` package (`plugin/pkg/client/auth/exec/`) caches credentials and re-executes the binary when they expire. This is the standard mechanism for cloud-provider authentication without embedding credentials.

**Risk**: If the exec binary is slow or unavailable, all API calls block waiting for credentials. The exec plugin runs in a goroutine per client, so deadlines on the parent context propagate, but errors surface as generic transport errors rather than auth-specific ones.

### 5.3 `TransformFunc` on Informers

`SharedInformer.SetTransform(handler TransformFunc)` (`tools/cache/shared_informer.go:231`) installs a function called on each object before it is inserted into the `Indexer`. Common uses:

- Stripping `managedFields` to reduce memory usage.
- Normalizing fields before indexing.
- Scrubbing sensitive data from the in-memory cache.

The transform runs synchronously in the `DeltaFIFO.Pop` path, under the FIFO lock. It must not access the informer's `Indexer` (deadlock risk) or perform slow I/O (back-pressure on the watch loop). Must be set before calling `Run()`.

### 5.4 Custom `Indexers`

Additional `IndexFunc` entries can be registered on a `SharedIndexInformer` via `AddIndexers()` before the informer starts (`tools/cache/index.go:58`). Custom indexes enable efficient lookups beyond namespace, such as indexing pods by node name or services by `spec.selector` labels. Each index function must be deterministic and cheap, since it runs on every object mutation.

**Risk**: Adding indexes after the informer starts returns an error. Index functions that panic will crash the goroutine processing that delta.

### 5.5 `WatchErrorHandler`

`SharedInformer.SetWatchErrorHandlerWithContext()` installs a callback invoked whenever `ListAndWatch` drops its connection. The default handler logs at different levels based on error type (`io.EOF` is silent, `io.ErrUnexpectedEOF` is logged at v1, others trigger `HandleError`). A custom handler can emit metrics or alerts. The handler runs in the Reflector's goroutine context and must return quickly.

### 5.6 Custom `WorkQueue` Backend

`TypedQueueConfig.Queue` (`util/workqueue/queue.go:98`) accepts an alternative queue implementation. The default is a slice-based FIFO. Custom implementations can support priority queuing (for example, preferring recently-changed objects) by implementing `Queue[T].Touch()`, which is called when a key that is already in the queue is added again.

### 5.7 `ResourceLock` Interface for Leader Election

`tools/leaderelection/resourcelock.Interface` defines the lock abstraction used by `LeaderElector`. The standard implementations use `Lease`, `ConfigMap`, or `Endpoints` Kubernetes objects. Custom implementations could use external systems (e.g., etcd directly or a database), provided they implement optimistic concurrency semantics.

### 5.8 Server-Side Apply and Generated `applyconfigurations`

For controllers that co-manage objects with other controllers or users, Server-Side Apply via the `applyconfigurations/` builders is strongly preferred over `get-modify-update`. The controller sets only the fields it owns; the server merges with fields owned by other managers. Custom field managers can be named per-controller.

**Risk**: Using full object `Update()` in a controller that coexists with Server-Side Apply will conflict with `managedFields` ownership. Mixing paradigms on the same object causes repeated update conflicts.

---

## 6. Operational Implications

### Performance

**Memory**: Each `SharedIndexInformer` holds a full in-memory copy of all objects of its type. A cluster with 100,000 pods, each 10 KB serialized, will use ~1 GB of cache per informer instance watching pods. `SetTransform` stripping `managedFields` and `status.conditions` can reduce this significantly (often 50-70% for pod objects).

**CPU**: `sharedProcessor` uses a ring buffer that avoids allocations per notification. `DeltaFIFO` uses a `sync.Cond` for producer/consumer coordination, avoiding busy-polling. The main CPU cost at steady state is JSON/protobuf decoding of watch events plus index maintenance.

**Network**: Each `SharedIndexInformer` opens one persistent HTTP/2 stream (watch connection). With HTTP/2 multiplexing, a single TCP connection can serve multiple watches. Protobuf (`application/vnd.kubernetes.protobuf`) is ~30-40% more compact than JSON and is the default for typed clients; this reduces bandwidth especially for large objects.

**Rate limiting**: The default `QPS=5, Burst=10` in `rest.Config` is appropriate for controllers doing occasional reconciliation. Controllers that list or update many objects per reconcile loop (e.g., managing 1,000 Pods per second) must raise these values, balancing against APF priority levels on the server.

### Reliability

**Watch reconnect**: The Reflector's exponential backoff (base 800ms, cap 30s, jitter) prevents stampede after an API server restart. After 2 minutes of stable operation the backoff resets, ensuring normal watch reconnection latency returns to near-zero.

**`HasSynced()`**: Controllers should gate reconciliation on `WaitForNamedCacheSync` or `WaitForNamedCacheSyncWithContext`. `HasSynced()` returns `true` only after the informer has completed its initial LIST. Acting before sync may cause controllers to re-create objects that already exist, generating unnecessary updates and potential conflicts.

**Handler panic recovery**: `sharedIndexInformer.RunWithContext` wraps with `utilruntime.HandleCrashWithContext`, which logs panics and continues running. However, panics inside user-supplied event handlers running in `processorListener.run` goroutines are not caught by this wrapper — they must be caught by the user, or the listener goroutine terminates silently and the handler stops receiving events.

### Maintainability

**Code generation**: The typed clients, listers, and informers in `kubernetes/`, `listers/`, and `informers/` are fully generated by `hack/update-codegen.sh`. Manual edits will be overwritten. Any modification to a built-in API type requires regenerating all three layers. Custom resource types follow the same pattern using `k8s.io/code-generator`.

**Feature gates**: Several behaviors are controlled by `k8s.io/client-go/features` gate checks at runtime. Notable gates: `WatchListClient` (streaming initialization), `ClientsAllowCBOR` / `ClientsPreferCBOR` (CBOR wire format), `InOrderInformersBatchProcess` (batch delta processing). Enabling these in production without testing in staging first can change memory and CPU behavior non-trivially.

---

## 7. Critical File Map

| Path | Role |
|------|------|
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Authoritative architectural overview from maintainers |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/rest/config.go` | `rest.Config` struct; `InClusterConfig()`; `RESTClientFor()`; rate limiter initialization |

| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/transport/round_trippers.go` | `HTTPWrappersForConfig()`; assembles auth/debug/user-agent/impersonation chain |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/dynamic/simple.go` | `DynamicClient`; `ConfigFor()` forcing JSON content type |
| `staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/listwatch.go` | `ListerWatcher`, `ListerWatcherWithContext` interfaces; `ListWatch`; `NewFilteredListWatchFromClient()` |
